author: @JudeTheYoutubePoopersubscribe
text: Have the a20+ which has a few minor upgrades on this. I charge it at work so it costs me absolutely nothing to run. I run it unlocked and it's great.

replies.author: @akihiro_power
replies.text: Do you have EU version or International version? Can we unlock the limit if it's EU ver.?

replies.author: @JudeTheYoutubePoopersubscribe
replies.text: @akihiro_power eu version has no throttle, which is the only way to get the bike to travel faster than 25km/h. On pedal assist it only goes to the legal limit. It'll do 32-35km/h on the international version using throttle only.

replies.author: @akihiro_power
replies.text: @JudeTheYoutubePoopersubscribe I get it. Thank you!

author: @AndreaDingbatt
text:
I am so lucky to have bought a 2nd hand E-bike, for £150.
The previous owner was a big bloke who had to buy a bigger E-bike, it is a load of fun!!
Instead of being a Disabled person, I become just another cyclist!!
I am going to have to buy a new battery, because I was really stupid and whist I was very ill, I lent it to my Ex-best friend....
Who swapped my almost New LiOn battery, with someone's Used battery...
Also, it has been knocked about a bit by him, so I am kicking myself up the Ass, for my Stooopidity!!
C'est La Vie! 🙂
Kindest Regards,
Andrea and Critter Family. XxX

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an amazing price! and oops - never lend what you mind losing

author: @SteampunkGent
text: Down in Cornwall I quite often fold up my e-bike and take it on local bus services - tho' some of the companies insist on you having it in a bag so it counts as hand luggage

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: in a bag - that's hilarious - though on second thoughts I can see the appeal - I wonder if there is a market for a bike in a suitcase?

replies.author: @SteampunkGent
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Bikes are not allowed on long distance trains without booking one of the 8 or so spaces provided; there is no charge. However unless your bike can be packed away it may not be allowed on the train. So a folded bike in a canvas carrier (which counts as hand luggage) needs no booked space. Interesting point is that, if your battery charger draws <100W then you can probably charge your battery!

author: @RaperJason
text: Good Review mate but your not fooling me the smile on your face every time you ride it tells me it is a Toy just one you will continue to use on a regular basis 😂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh it's great fun mate!

author: @danrawson8515
text: Does it charge up when cycling?

replies.author: @SteampunkGent
replies.text: Unfortunately very few E-Bikes have regenerative braking. Part of the reason seems to be that the process is very inefficient. There was a video where an E-biker estimated it only gave 2 or 3 extra miles on a journey of 20, which with the added weight of the mechanism made it not worth the investment

replies.author: @fender1000100
replies.text:
It does give you back some power with the G force regenerative braking system. Everything comes down to the SPEED you ride at. Your weight and terrain you go on.

I have range testerd it on all five PAS and throttle levels.

PAS 1=43 MILES
PAS 2=36 MILES
PAS 3=27 MILES
PAS 4 (THROTTLE)=21 MILES
PAS 5 (THROTTLE)=17 MILES

PAS 1=9MPH
PAS 2=12MPH
PAS 3=15 MPH
PAS 4=17 MPH
PAS 5=20MPH

When you unlock the A20.

So the faster you go obviously the trade off is how much voltage is consumed.

Fully charged I have 41.1 VOLTS
And I set the cut off at 28.5 VOLTS.

The regenerative system really kicks in once you are down to around 36V. I average 21 miles a day on my commute to and from work. Getting to work will take me down to 36.5V on average. Getting home will take me down to 34V on average. So less VOLTS are consumed on the home trip. Which makes sense as there are more hills and uphill grades going to work. When using the THROTTLE you will see the voltage draw down alot. But as soon as you come off it. It bounces back up to it's TRUE current level. It can fully charge a mobile phone in about an hour and 10 minutes. And this will only cost you about 0.3 to 0.5 of a volt. It's a truly FANTASTIC BIKE. 750 euros or less in some places does not buy a better bike with so many features and performance. I promise you.

replies.author: @SteampunkGent
replies.text: @fender1000100 Thanks, I've obviously not kept up with regen. braking. The speed/mass limitation are particularly applicable to me as I'm just coming up to 70. I can only really pedal up  to about 12 mph on my 21" wheels and only approach 15+ downhill on limited sections BUT using  RB will lose impetus for the next uphill section - this is Cornwall after all.

author: @GreenJimll
text: A nice looking bike, and good to get a review of day-to-day "normal" (for me!) usage, rather than lycra clad road warriors or tough mountain bikers.  I'm wondering how easy it would be to add some kind of rack. My (non-electric, ex-skip) bicycle has a wire basket at the front and a rear rack with panniers, and both get used regularly as I use it to shift stuff as well me around.

author: @strawman9410
text:
Recently built my mountain bike into an E-Bike for the same thing, to make getting around easier. I got a 10ah battery. With a range of less than 10 mile ! Sounds like you're getting close to 32 mile. So my next upgrades will include a bigger battery.
I remember a while back you were building a battery for an E-car. How did that go ? I missed the ending of that project.

replies.author: @SteampunkGent
replies.text:
I use a 14 ah/35v battery. This has got me to Penzance (25 miles on Cycle Route 3) and ⅔ of the way back so about 40 miles. I could (just) have made the entire journey, I think, but my knees were playing up - see my previous comment on taking on busses.

Can I make a guess and say it sounds like you have a front wheel motor?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: around 40 miles which is pretty good - the battery project is still ongoing
